feed.title: Dylan Ale
feed.subtitle: I'm an analyst, sailor, & trader near SF. ghpages+jekyll+minima+chivo/inconsolata

entry.title: Quant: Inside n Up
entry.content:
Here I try to find inside and up bars on the daily timeframe.

Fetch the data from BarChart

We call the barchart getHistory API. This is adapted from BlackArb.

Steps:

  • import libraries
  • set locations & key
  • build URL
  • function
  • call function
#----------IMPORT LIBRARIES----------#
import time
t0 = time.clock()
import pandas as pd
from pandas.tseries.offsets import BDay
import numpy as np
import datetime as dt
from copy import copy
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore',category=pd.io.pytables.PerformanceWarning)
 
    
#----------SET DIRECTORIES----------#
    
project_dir = r'C:\Users/drale/Desktop/python/' 
price_path = project_dir + r'Stock_Price_Data\\'

components = pd.read_excel(project_dir +\
                             'assets/Watchlist.xls', header=0)
syms = components.Identifier.dropna()
syms = syms.drop(syms.index[-1]).sort_values()


apikey = '99f7186682f41a3bd739984105aef52a'


#----------BUILD URL----------#

def construct_barChart_url(sym, start_date, freq, api_key=apikey):
    
    url = 'http://marketdata.websol.barchart.com/getHistory.csv?' +\
            'key={}&symbol={}&type={}&startDate={}'.format(api_key, sym, freq, start_date)
    return url

#----------F(X) DEFINITION----------#

def get_minute_data():
    
    # YYYY MM DD HH MM SS
    start = '20170101'#'20160101000000'
    #end = d
    freq = 'daily'    
    prices = {}
    symbol_count = len(syms)
    
    try:
        for i, sym in enumerate(syms, start=1):
            api_url = construct_barChart_url(sym, start, freq, api_key=apikey)
            try:
                csvfile = pd.read_csv(api_url, parse_dates=['timestamp'])
                csvfile.set_index('timestamp', inplace=True)
                prices[sym] = csvfile
            except:
                continue

            print('{}..[got] | {}/{} tickers |'.format(sym, i, symbol_count)) 
    except Exception as e: 
        print(e)
    finally:
        pass
    
    px = pd.Panel.from_dict(prices)
    px.major_axis = px.major_axis.tz_localize('utc').tz_convert('US/Pacific')
    return px

#----------CALL FUNCTION----------#
pxx = get_minute_data()
pxx


# timer
secs      = np.round( ( time.clock()  - t0 ), 4 )
time_secs = "{timeSecs} seconds to run".format(timeSecs = secs)
print( time_secs )

AAPL..[got] | 1/18 tickers |
ACIA..[got] | 2/18 tickers |
AMZN..[got] | 3/18 tickers |
BABA..[got] | 4/18 tickers |
BIDU..[got] | 5/18 tickers |
FB..[got] | 6/18 tickers |
GILD..[got] | 7/18 tickers |
GOOGL..[got] | 8/18 tickers |
GS..[got] | 9/18 tickers |
IBB..[got] | 10/18 tickers |
INTC..[got] | 11/18 tickers |
JD..[got] | 12/18 tickers |
JPM..[got] | 13/18 tickers |
NFLX..[got] | 14/18 tickers |
NVDA..[got] | 15/18 tickers |
PANW..[got] | 16/18 tickers |
SQ..[got] | 17/18 tickers |
TSLA..[got] | 18/18 tickers |
21.8674 seconds to run

Analysis

stock = 'TSLA'
ohlc = pxx[stock]

#higher highs and lower lows
ohlc["higher"] = np.where((ohlc.high - ohlc.high.shift(1)) > 0, True, False)
ohlc["lower"]  = np.where((ohlc.low  - ohlc.low.shift(1))  < 0, True, False)

#inside and outside bars
ohlc["outside"]  = np.logical_and(ohlc.higher==True, ohlc.lower==True)
ohlc["inside"]   = np.logical_and(ohlc.higher==False, ohlc.lower==False)

#buy where previous inside bar and current higher highs
ohlc["buy"]  = np.logical_and(ohlc.inside ==True, ohlc.higher.shift(1) ==True)

# sell where close < previous lows
ohlc["sell"] = np.where((ohlc["close"] < ohlc["low"].shift(1)), True, False)

ohlc[["high", "low", "close", "higher", "lower", "inside", "outside", "buy", "sell"]].head(10)
high low close higher lower inside outside buy sell
timestamp
2017-01-02 21:00:00-08:00 220.33 210.96 216.99 False False True False False False
2017-01-03 21:00:00-08:00 228 214.31 226.99 True False False False False False
2017-01-04 21:00:00-08:00 227.48 221.95 226.75 False False True False True False
2017-01-05 21:00:00-08:00 230.31 225.45 229.01 True False False False False False
2017-01-08 21:00:00-08:00 231.92 228 231.28 True False False False False False
2017-01-09 21:00:00-08:00 232 226.89 229.87 True True False True False False
2017-01-10 21:00:00-08:00 229.98 226.68 229.73 False True False False False False
2017-01-11 21:00:00-08:00 230.7 225.58 229.59 True True False True False False
2017-01-12 21:00:00-08:00 237.85 229.59 237.75 True False False False False False
2017-01-16 21:00:00-08:00 239.96 234.37 235.58 True False False False False False

Backtest

pnl = 0
change = 0
pos = False

log = pd.DataFrame()


for index, row in ohlc.iterrows(): 
    
    if row.buy == True & pos == False:
        pos = True
        entry = row.close

        log.set_value(index,'pos', pos)
        log.set_value(index,'close',row['close'])
        log.set_value(index,'pnl',pnl)

    if row.sell == True:

        pos = False
        pnl = pnl + row['close'] - entry

        log.set_value(index,'pos', pos)
        log.set_value(index,'close',row['close'])
        log.set_value(index,'pnl',pnl)

    
log.head(50)
#log.sort_values(by='change',ascending=0)
pos close pnl
2017-01-02 21:00:00-08:00 True 216.99 0.00
2017-02-15 21:00:00-08:00 False 268.95 51.96
2017-02-16 21:00:00-08:00 True 272.23 51.96
2017-02-21 21:00:00-08:00 False 273.51 53.24
2017-02-22 21:00:00-08:00 False 255.99 53.24
2017-02-23 21:00:00-08:00 True 257.00 53.24
2017-02-26 21:00:00-08:00 False 246.23 42.47
2017-02-27 21:00:00-08:00 True 249.99 42.47
2017-03-07 21:00:00-08:00 False 246.87 39.35
2017-03-08 21:00:00-08:00 False 244.90 39.35
2017-03-09 21:00:00-08:00 True 243.69 39.35
2017-03-20 21:00:00-07:00 False 250.68 46.34
2017-03-22 21:00:00-07:00 True 254.78 46.34
2017-04-11 21:00:00-07:00 False 296.84 88.40
2017-04-12 21:00:00-07:00 True 304.00 88.40
2017-04-26 21:00:00-07:00 False 308.63 93.03
2017-04-27 21:00:00-07:00 True 314.07 93.03
2017-05-02 21:00:00-07:00 False 311.02 89.98
2017-05-03 21:00:00-07:00 False 295.46 89.98
2017-05-04 21:00:00-07:00 True 308.35 89.98
2017-05-14 21:00:00-07:00 False 315.88 97.51
2017-05-15 21:00:00-07:00 True 317.01 97.51
2017-05-16 21:00:00-07:00 False 306.11 86.61
2017-05-17 21:00:00-07:00 True 313.06 86.61
2017-05-22 21:00:00-07:00 False 303.86 77.41
2017-05-23 21:00:00-07:00 True 310.22 77.41
2017-06-08 21:00:00-07:00 False 357.32 124.51
2017-06-11 21:00:00-07:00 True 359.01 124.51
2017-06-14 21:00:00-07:00 False 375.34 140.84
2017-06-15 21:00:00-07:00 True 371.40 140.84
2017-06-18 21:00:00-07:00 False 369.80 139.24
2017-06-19 21:00:00-07:00 True 372.24 139.24
2017-06-25 21:00:00-07:00 False 377.49 144.49
2017-06-26 21:00:00-07:00 False 362.37 144.49
2017-06-27 21:00:00-07:00 True 371.24 144.49
2017-06-28 21:00:00-07:00 False 360.75 134.00
2017-06-29 21:00:00-07:00 True 361.61 134.00
2017-07-02 21:00:00-07:00 False 352.62 125.01
2017-07-04 21:00:00-07:00 False 327.09 125.01
2017-07-05 21:00:00-07:00 False 308.83 125.01
2017-07-06 21:00:00-07:00 True 313.22 125.01
2017-07-12 21:00:00-07:00 False 323.41 135.20
2017-07-13 21:00:00-07:00 True 327.78 135.20
2017-07-16 21:00:00-07:00 False 319.57 126.99
2017-07-17 21:00:00-07:00 True 328.24 126.99
2017-07-26 21:00:00-07:00 False 334.46 133.21
2017-07-30 21:00:00-07:00 False 323.47 133.21
2017-07-31 21:00:00-07:00 False 319.57 133.21
2017-08-01 21:00:00-07:00 True 325.89 133.21
2017-08-09 21:00:00-07:00 False 355.40 162.72

Chart

Line chart of price action with green dots for entries and yellow dots for exits, generated with matplotlib.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

#exposes fig and ax components of the plot (for modification)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1)

start = '2017-01-01'
end = '2017-9-01'

cut = ohlc.ix[start:end]
plt.plot(cut.index, cut.close, 'burlywood', linewidth=3)

buy_list = log[(log.pos == True)]
exit_list = log[(log.pos == False)]
buy_list = buy_list.ix[start:end]
exit_list = exit_list.ix[start:end]
x= buy_list.index
x1= exit_list.index
y= buy_list['close']
y1= exit_list['close']

plt.plot(x,y,'c^', x1,y1,'rv', markersize=5)

plt.title(stock + " trades, " + start + " to " + end)
plt.grid(True)
fig.autofmt_xdate()

plt.show()
entry.summary: Here I try to find inside and up bars on the daily timeframe.

entry.title: When Breath Becomes Air
entry.content:
I recently purchased “When Breath Becomes Air” by Paul Kalanithi on Google Books before a return flight from New Zealand, and read it in a couple days. It is a short story about Paul’s career as a neurosurgeon, and his subsequent battle with cancer.

I liked this book because it gives a circumspect view of mortality, both from the 3rd-person medical understanding, and the 1st-person experiential understanding. Knowing what death is like before facing it yourself allows you to live life more deliberately, and to arrive at your own end peacefully. It is an uncomfortable and sad look into the reality that each and every one of us will face. I could not stop reading - it is the first book I have finished to completion in the past couple years.

Summary

Paul wrote the story as he died from lung cancer, so it is to-the-point and direct. His perspective is specifically valuable because he had the capability and aspirations to become a writer, but was pulled into medicine by his desire to understand what makes a life meaningful. He was a constant philosopher, wondering at hard questions and striving to be the best, putting himself in situations which allowed organic understanding of life’s hardest questions. He realized, as a doctor, he could provide direct value to patients grappling with life’s final outcome - their own mortality.

As a doctor he would take responsibility for the patient’s mortality, hiding information as necessary and providing only the level of accuracy that he could be sure of. He would never say “you have 6 months to live,” after all who was he to say? Instead, he would say that many people tend to live several years. In framing the survival curve, he provided hope, comfort, and stability to people on the precipice of falling apart.

Paul contracted lung cancer just as he approached the end of his medical residency, as a shoe-in to a highly coveted neuroscience role at Stanford, combining practice with research. After striving towards his goal for decades, his life was abruptly halted by the very thing he specialized in. As the symptoms manifested in Paul, he gained a different understanding and appreciation for his patients’ experience.

Thoughts

It is valuable to experience death before it comes for you because this allows you to “being with the end in mind.” This reality of life is often ignored, as is is uncomfortable and hard to handle. What is the point of living if we are going to die? What constitutes a meaningful life during our time here? The earlier you can ask these questions, the more likely you are to be fulfilled.

Of course, it is possible at any time that, like Paul, your life may be cut short and your search for meaning may come up with no results. Life is not fair, and this is another aspect that the book drives home. There is no great arbiter, who doles out reward for input. Life is random, and diseases - especially cancer - is cruel and unfair. The coping mechanisms and mindsets that result are to be understood and accounted for. Dying of cancer is painful and unsightly. Paul’s body crashed, with tumors eventually limiting his ability to breath to the point where he was on a breather and unable to move. Paul certainly did not deserve this. He faced his problems with grace nonetheless, and was able to call an audible and provide value to the world in a unique way - by sharing his experience with the world.

I am sure you have people in your life that you are close to that have experienced or are experiencing cancer. Personally this book gave me greater sympathy with their struggles, and allowed me to put myself in their shoes. It is a scary and dark thing. I hope now I can be more present and available to these people. I also hope I am spurred to action, to not rest on my laurels. We do not have unlimited time. We may not even have as much time as we think. Be grateful for each day. Do what you want and provide value however you can. In the end, make your life worth living. It is perhaps the most important thing.
entry.summary: I recently purchased “When Breath Becomes Air” by Paul Kalanithi on Google Books before a return flight from New Zealand, and read it in a couple days. It is a short story about Paul’s career as a neurosurgeon, and his subsequent battle with cancer.

entry.title: Quant: Trend
entry.content:
Here I measure trends to find potential buy spots. This analysis compares sequential bar highs/lows to assert trend reversals.

This is an analysis from a Python Jupyter notebook, exported directly. The tables below each code block are the API responses.

I am using the BarChart.com OnDemand API to call getHistory() and receive 3 months of minute data for a basket of stocks.

To use the code yourself, you will need to setup a python environment, jupyter notebook or otherwise. Then request a free BarChart.com OnDemand API key, and plug it in.

Last, set the file path for your project directory, and in that folder create a folder ‘Stock_Price_Data’ to export data to, and a folder ‘assets’ for your list. In ‘assets’ create an Excel file ‘Watchlist’. With a header have names in the first column and tickers in the second column.

Fetch the data from BarChart

Here we call the barchart getHistory API. This is an adaptation of BlackArb’s Python tutorial.

Steps:

  • import libraries
  • set directories and API key
  • build URL to call API
  • get_minute_data() function
  • call the function
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

#----------IMPORT LIBRARIES----------#
import time
t0 = time.clock()

import pandas as pd
from pandas.tseries.offsets import BDay

import numpy as np
import datetime as dt

from copy import copy
import warnings
warnings.filterwarnings('ignore',category=pd.io.pytables.PerformanceWarning)
 

    
#----------SET DIRECTORIES----------#
    
project_dir = r'C:\Users/drale/Desktop/python/' 
price_path = project_dir + r'Stock_Price_Data\\'

components = pd.read_excel(project_dir +\
                             'assets/Watchlist.xls', header=0)
syms = components.Identifier.dropna()
syms = syms.drop(syms.index[-1]).sort_values()


apikey = 'x'



#----------BUILD URL----------#

def construct_barChart_url(sym, start_date, freq, api_key=apikey):
    '''Function to construct barchart api url'''
    
    url = 'http://marketdata.websol.barchart.com/getHistory.csv?' +\
            'key={}&symbol={}&type={}&startDate={}'.format(api_key, sym, freq, start_date)
    return url

#outputs: symbol, timestamp, tradingDay, open, high, low, close, volume, openInterest

#----------F(X) DEFINITION----------#

def get_minute_data():
    '''Function to Retrieve <= 3 months of minute data'''
    
    # YYYY MM DD HH MM SS
    start = '20171001000000'
    #end = d
    freq = 'minutes'    
    prices = {}
    symbol_count = len(syms)
    
    try:
        for i, sym in enumerate(syms, start=1):
            api_url = construct_barChart_url(sym, start, freq, api_key=apikey)
            try:
                csvfile = pd.read_csv(api_url, parse_dates=['timestamp'])
                csvfile.set_index('timestamp', inplace=True)
                prices[sym] = csvfile
            except:
                continue

            print('{}..[fetched] | {} of {} tickers |'.format(sym, i, symbol_count)) 
    except Exception as e: 
        print(e)
    finally:
        pass
    
    px = pd.Panel.from_dict(prices)
    px.major_axis = px.major_axis.tz_localize('utc').tz_convert('US/Pacific')
    return px



#----------CALL FUNCTION----------#

pxx = get_minute_data()
pxx


# timer
secs      = np.round( ( time.clock()  - t0 ), 4 )
time_secs = "{timeSecs} seconds to run".format(timeSecs = secs)
print( time_secs )

AAPL..[fetched] | 1 of 9 tickers |
AMZN..[fetched] | 2 of 9 tickers |
BIDU..[fetched] | 3 of 9 tickers |
FB..[fetched] | 4 of 9 tickers |
GOOGL..[fetched] | 5 of 9 tickers |
GS..[fetched] | 6 of 9 tickers |
IBB..[fetched] | 7 of 9 tickers |
NFLX..[fetched] | 8 of 9 tickers |
TSLA..[fetched] | 9 of 9 tickers |
46.9206 seconds to run

Select stock

Choose a ticker to analyze, and test the output.

stock = 'GOOGL'

ohlc = pxx[stock]
ohlc.tail(1)
symbol tradingDay open high low close volume
timestamp
2017-11-09 13:00:00-08:00 GOOGL 2017-11-09 1047.72 1047.72 1047.72 1047.72 68623

Adjust data type from object to numeric

ohlc['open'] = pd.to_numeric(ohlc['open'], errors='coerce')
ohlc['high'] = pd.to_numeric(ohlc['high'], errors='coerce')
ohlc['low'] = pd.to_numeric(ohlc['low'], errors='coerce')
ohlc['close'] = pd.to_numeric(ohlc['close'], errors='coerce')
#ohlc['volume'] = pd.to_numeric(ohlc['volume'], errors='coerce')

ohlc.info()

<class 'pandas.core.frame.DataFrame'>
DatetimeIndex: 11339 entries, 2017-10-02 06:30:00-07:00 to 2017-11-09 13:00:00-08:00
Data columns (total 7 columns):
symbol        11055 non-null object
tradingDay    11055 non-null object
open          11055 non-null float64
high          11055 non-null float64
low           11055 non-null float64
close         11055 non-null float64
volume        11055 non-null object
dtypes: float64(4), object(3)
memory usage: 708.7+ KB

Resample timeframe

Roll up minute data to custom timeframe. In this instance, it is 1 hour. We take the first open, the max high, the min low, and the last close.

timeframe = '60T'

columns = ['open','high','low','close']
tf = pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)

tf['open'] = ohlc['open'].resample(timeframe, how='first')
tf['high'] = ohlc['high'].resample(timeframe, how='max')
tf['low'] = ohlc['low'].resample(timeframe, how='min')
tf['close'] = ohlc['close'].resample(timeframe, how='last')

tf = tf[np.isfinite(tf['high'])] #clear NaNs
tf.tail(2)
open high low close
timestamp
2017-11-09 12:00:00-08:00 1044.01 1049.33 1042.80 1047.54
2017-11-09 13:00:00-08:00 1047.72 1047.72 1047.72 1047.72

Trend

  • See if timeframe is making new highs, new lows
  • Return 1 for uptrend or downtrend
  • If new trend starting, flag as buyable or sellable.
#calculate recent difference in highs and lows
tf["highd"] = tf.high - tf.high.shift(1)
tf["lowd"] = tf.low.shift(1) - tf.low 

#uptrend means higher highs, downtrend means lower lows
tf["up_tr"] = np.where(tf["highd"] > 0, 1, 0)
tf["down_tr"] = np.where(tf["lowd"] > 0, 1, 0)

#h/h and l/l means expansion, l/h and h/l means contraction (not currently used)
tf["exp"] = np.where((tf["highd"]>0) & (tf["lowd"]>0),1,0)
tf["cont"] = np.where((tf["highd"]<0) & (tf["lowd"]<0),1,0)

#where new uptrend established, buyable.. new downtrend sellable
tf["buy"] = np.where((tf["up_tr"] == 1) & (tf["up_tr"].shift(1) == 0), 1, 0)
tf["sell"] = np.where((tf["down_tr"] == 1) & (tf["down_tr"].shift(1) == 0), 1, 0)

tf[["high", "low", "close", "highd", "lowd", "up_tr", "down_tr", "exp", "cont", "buy", "sell"]].tail(5)
high low close highd lowd up_tr down_tr exp cont buy sell
timestamp
2017-11-09 09:00:00-08:00 1043.025 1036.54 1038 -3.465 3.86 0 1 0 0 0 1
2017-11-09 10:00:00-08:00 1040.300 1035.85 1038.63 -2.725 0.69 0 1 0 0 0 0
2017-11-09 11:00:00-08:00 1044.420 1038.60 1043.77 4.120 -2.75 1 0 0 0 1 0
2017-11-09 12:00:00-08:00 1049.330 1042.80 1047.54 4.910 -4.20 1 0 0 0 0 0
2017-11-09 13:00:00-08:00 1047.720 1047.72 1047.72 -1.610 -4.92 0 0 0 1 0 0

Backtest

Loop through the timeframe. If new buy signal, go long. If new sell signal, go short.
1 share long on long signal, and 1 share short on short signal. This is not additive. If there’s a short on, and a long signal is received, you fully close the short and open the long.

Log the backtest: long or short, closing price (executed price), and a running total.

# PUSHING TO LOG[] DATAFRAME
total = 0
buys = 0
sells = 0
prev = 0

columns = ['LS','price_close', 'total']
log = pd.DataFrame(columns=columns)

for index, row in tf.iterrows():

    if row['buy'] > 0 and buys == 0:
        buys = 1
        sells = 0
        if prev > 0: total = total - row['close'] + prev
        log.set_value(index,'LS','long')
        log.set_value(index,'price_close',row['close'])
        log.set_value(index,'total',total)
        prev = row['close']

    if row['sell'] == 1 and sells == 0:
        sells = 1
        buys = 0
        if prev > 0: total = total + row['close'] - prev
        log.set_value(index,'LS','short')
        log.set_value(index,'price_close',row['close'])
        log.set_value(index,'total',total)
        prev = row['close']
    
log.tail(25)
#log.sort_values(by='total',ascending=0)
LS price_close total
2017-10-30 11:00:00-07:00 short 1032.04 35.45
2017-10-31 08:00:00-07:00 long 1032.62 34.865
2017-10-31 09:00:00-07:00 short 1032.26 34.505
2017-10-31 10:00:00-07:00 long 1033.98 32.785
2017-10-31 12:00:00-07:00 short 1033.05 31.855
2017-11-01 06:00:00-07:00 long 1037.74 27.165
2017-11-01 10:00:00-07:00 short 1044.66 34.085
2017-11-02 06:00:00-07:00 short 1039.78 38.965
2017-11-02 09:00:00-07:00 long 1039.62 39.125
2017-11-02 11:00:00-07:00 short 1044.3 43.805
2017-11-03 08:00:00-07:00 long 1044 44.105
2017-11-06 06:00:00-08:00 short 1050.01 50.11
2017-11-06 10:00:00-08:00 long 1047.86 52.255
2017-11-06 11:00:00-08:00 short 1046.08 50.475
2017-11-07 06:00:00-08:00 long 1047.07 49.482
2017-11-07 08:00:00-08:00 short 1044.14 46.549
2017-11-07 09:00:00-08:00 long 1051.23 39.459
2017-11-07 11:00:00-08:00 short 1048.72 36.949
2017-11-07 12:00:00-08:00 long 1052.39 33.279
2017-11-07 13:00:00-08:00 short 1052.39 33.279
2017-11-08 07:00:00-08:00 long 1052.64 33.031
2017-11-08 12:00:00-08:00 short 1058.59 38.983
2017-11-09 08:00:00-08:00 long 1041.76 55.813
2017-11-09 09:00:00-08:00 short 1038 52.053
2017-11-09 11:00:00-08:00 long 1043.77 46.281

Last week’s trades

Line chart of price action with green dots for buys and red dots for sells, generated with matplotlib.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from matplotlib.finance import candlestick2_ohlc

#exposes fig and ax components of the plot (for modification)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1)

start = '2017-10-30 06:30:00'
end = '2017-11-03 13:30:00'

cut = tf.ix[start:end]

#candlestick2_ohlc(ax, cut['open'], cut['high'], cut['low'], cut['close'], width=0.6)
ax.plot(cut.index, cut.close)


buy_list = log[(log.LS == 'long')]
buy_list = buy_list.ix[start:end]
x= buy_list.index
y= buy_list['price_close']
plt.plot(x,y, 'go')

sell_list = log[(log.LS == 'short')]
sell_list = sell_list.ix[start:end]
x1= sell_list.index
y1= sell_list['price_close']
plt.plot(x1,y1, 'ro')


import matplotlib.dates as mdates
ax.fmt_xdata = mdates.DateFormatter('%Y-%m-%d')
plt.title('buys and sells')
ax.grid(True)
fig.autofmt_xdate()

plt.show()
entry.summary: Here I measure trends to find potential buy spots. This analysis compares sequential bar highs/lows to assert trend reversals.

entry.title: Driverless effect on traffic
entry.content:
I recently moved to San Jose, so I have to commute on the 87 and 101. On the way home, the merge from 101 to 87 is particularly atrocious.

It’s a right side combined on-ramp/off-ramp. The on-ramp Trimble Rd -> 101 becomes the offramp 101 -> 87. So cars are exiting the lane at the same time cars are entering the lane. This combined use of a single lane creates bumper to bumper traffic.

There are two approaches to merging from 101 to 87.

Polite southbound commuters will merge ASAP, to allow traffic continuing on 101 past 87 to flow.

Some jerks zoom forward in the 2nd right lane to skip the 97 lineup.
Usually these people wedge themselves in too late, slowing the whole merge lane to a stop.
Other times they are stuck stopped in the middle of the freeway with their blinker on, as the cars already in the right lane prevent them from merging. (ostensibly to teach them a lesson)

It’s a huge mess, partially due to the road design and partially due to driver behavior. I can’t help but think of a self-driving future.

My questions are:

  • Will self-driving cars drive aggressively to the detriment of the whole?

  • Or will they only do what’s best for the group? -If beta by design, will regular cars be able to drive even more aggressively?

  • Will self-driving aggression be user-controlled?
    • If user-controlled, will there be incentives to keep levels low? Wouldn’t everyone set the aggression at 10/10? (related to insurance premiums)
  • Or decided by the car?
    • If car-controlled, will each car be competing on time or other convenience factors?

Kinda looking forward to their debut, kinda not. I’m afraid the future will be frustrating too, but in a different way, where all our cars are unwilling to just scoot ahead.

Check out this Fortune article about how Google is humanizing their cars.

I have heard the theory that blockchain will enable self-driving cars to have their own ledger, and be incentivized to seek out gas, provide cheaper rates, etc. in pursuit of capital accumulation. In this case, I am sure these cars won’t be “shy.”
entry.summary: I recently moved to San Jose, so I have to commute on the 87 and 101. On the way home, the merge from 101 to 87 is particularly atrocious.

entry.title: Camping in Mendocino County
entry.content:
Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

Jake, Brian, and I camped at Van Damme State Park Friday and Saturday night. (Van Damme, gurl)

It was a 5hr drive from San Jose in rush hour traffic! Luckily we did take the scenic routes closer to the coast, which ended up being further, but without traffic and along 2 lane roads. As Google Maps tends to adjust ETA adding up to half an hour as traffic rises, we definitely saved time thinking through our route.

The site was #37 and was situated nicely on top of a rise, with bathrooms nearby and good sunlight in the morning.

We kept meals to a couple ingredients to save space and reduce costs:

  • Pasta (tortellini, alfredo)
  • Smores (graham crackers, marshmallows, hershey’s)
  • Quesadillas (tortillas, mexican cheese, sausage)
  • Oatmeal (oats, peanut butter)
  • Mandarin Oranges

Our original plan was to take a sea cave kayaking tour, but it was expensive and full. So we called an audible and drove up to Fort Bragg. Here’s the places we went:

  • Glass Beach, to see the glass and swim. Cold water filled with kelp forests! The Van Damme camp ranger told us Glass Beach used to be a literal dump, then the county was told to clean it up. All the broken bottles became glass beach. Now that’s good marketing. We talked to an abalone diver who was tagging some shells, and he explained he was part of a competition taking place today.

  • Caspar Beach, where a Triton Free Diving competition weigh in was going on. We saw abalone, cod, and scallops the size of a baseball mitt.

  • North Coast Brewery, home of Brother Thelonius Belgian Style Abbey Ale (appley) and the Rasputin Russian Imperial Stout (chocolatey).

  • Pygmy Forest Trail. Pygmy means short, and this forest’s stunted growth is caused by poor soil, as the area sat underwater many years ago. There were huckleberrys (very edible!) along the boardwalk through the pygmy forest, followed by a wide fire trail to the next intersection about 2 1/2 miles up, surrounded by redwoods.

  • Big River beach, to enjoy a beer and catch the sunset after the hike. The sun sets over the point instead of the ocean, so you get to enjoy the flash of the sun setting, then a few extra minutes of light to finish your beer as the actual sunset occurs. The area in the back, a broad beach along the big river, looked excellent for hanging out during the day. (noted)

  • Russian River Brewery, home of Pliny the Elder/Younger. A great pit stop on the way back. It was very busy with locals on Sunday as it is happy hour all day, so we ate down the street at a burger joint and came back. I tried the Golden Ale and grabbed a growler of Pliny.
entry.summary: Before this next weekend gets started, I wanted to get the details of my trip to Mendocino on paper.

entry.title: The guys @ Finless Foods
entry.content:
I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

Zois -my housemate Ian’s friend from time in Chile- stayed on our couch for a few days. He invited me to Massive Attack at 1015 Folsom with Emily, a friend visiting SF. Emily was staying in SoMa with Mike, who she met in Taiwan.

Mike lives with Brian, and together they run Finless Foods, a new food startup creating fish without fishing or farming. They’re growing fish meat from stem cells. Finless is creating surimi (fish paste) in the lab now.

  • Their fish is healthier than caught fish which contains mercury and plastic.
  • It is also healthier than farmed fish which contains antibiotics and hormones.
  • As the fish is grown without a body of water, production can be geographically distributed.
  • Since no fish are harmed, Finless appeals to ethical vegans.

An interesting question my housemate Ian asked is: can this fish be produced in a way that is less carbon intensive than the usual process?

When Zois and I picked up Mike, Brian, and co-worker Jihyun, they had just wrapped up an initial tasting. “This is three quarters of the company in the backseat, right now!” they joked as they piled in. Mike was looking forward to “only” having to speak in front of 1000 people the following week. Easy, in comparison.

Zois, Emily, Mike, and I went to Massive Attack together. It was a fun show, closing with “Teardrop”. Afterwards, we took a peek at the IndieBio incubator which was right around the corner.

In the morning, Mike mentioned that he had actually felt unstressed for the first time in months. Balance is important! (An hour later he was off to New York on a plane.)

Here’s the link to the “1000 people” talk mentioned above - IndieBio SF Demo Day 5.

Mike comes on just before the 1:03:00 mark.

Good luck to Finless Foods!!
entry.summary: I met some cool people in a roundabout way last week.

entry.title: Why do blogs die?
entry.content:
Blogs are everywhere. You probably have an old one under your bed somewhere.

They’re started by teachers, travellers, investors, product people and developers.

They’re hosted on Blogger, Wordpress, or Tumblr. Or on Squarespace, Ghost, Wix, Weebly, etc.

They’re painstakenly designed, categorized, and paletted.

Then there’s 5 posts total and the most recent one is from over 2 years ago. It’s tragic really. So much intent and good will, stopped dead in its tracks.

Here’s this person who put in a ton of platform work to connect with the world, and you can just see how quickly it fizzled and died.

Why?

A) People make blogs about stuff they want to be an expert in, not about stuff they’re already experts in. B) Turns out, it’s tiring to try to be something you’re not.

The author is neither writing for themselves nor from actual knowledge. They’re writing to some pie-in-the-sky audience and putting on a persona to attract them.

It gets harder to write over time because there’s no new ideas being discussed, only regurgitation.

This ecosystem of B.S is particularly obvious with LinkedIn published articles lately.

A job seeker mashes his keyboard for a few hours to create a listicle (5 ways to be more productive at work). Upon reading it he thinks to himself in horror, “What did I just write?” Given the huge sunk cost of having written the article, he shrugs and hits SUBMIT, figuring some actual professional will be able to understand what he’s getting at.
Later a reader clicks on and skims through the article, thinking to himself, “What is this that I’m reading?” Confused and wasted, he shrugs and hits LIKE, figuring someone will see his like and think he understood it.

We can all agree the internet doesn’t need any more of that kind of stuff. It’s wasting everyone’s time.

So guess what? This is going to be the exact opposite.

I’m going to write randomly and colloquially, and only about things that I understand and that interest me. There will be no standard length post, and no general topic. Posts will be edited over time.

Buckle up!
entry.summary: Blogs are everywhere. You probably have an old one under your bed somewhere.
